studyMetadata.title: Grasp Explorer: AI-in-the-Loop Visualization Evaluation
studyMetadata.description: Three-section evaluation of the Grasp Explorer tool covering the landscape view, AI copilot, and analytics dashboard. Includes screen and audio recording for think-aloud analysis.

consent.instruction: Before we begin, please confirm the following and tell us about your background.

response.prompt: I consent to having my screen and audio recorded during this study.
What best describes your role?
Years of experience in robotics research?
How familiar are you with robotic grasp planning?
How familiar are you with AI/ML concepts (embeddings, clustering, LLMs)?

section1-explore.instruction:
**Section 1: Explore the scatter plot and clustering.**

Do NOT type a query yet. Just look at the default view:
- Hover over points to see method names
- Look at the cluster colors and labels
- **Click a cluster label in the legend** to filter
- Try the **Color by** dropdown to see different attributes
- Try dragging a **weight slider** — watch how the scatter plot reorganizes
- Click a method to see its details

**Think aloud**: describe what you see and whether the groupings make sense.

**Note:** You won't have access to the tool while answering questions next, so take a good look now.

When done, click **Next**.

section1-feedback.instruction: Questions about the scatter plot and clustering.

response.prompt: The clustering of methods into groups was meaningful and made sense to me.
The clustering revealed groupings or relationships I had not considered before.
The spatial layout (which methods are near/far) matched my intuition about method similarity.
The cluster labels (e.g., 'Sampling / Two-finger / Piled') were easy to understand.
Were there any methods grouped together that surprised you, or methods you expected to be grouped that were not?
response.placeholder: Describe any surprising or missing groupings...

response.prompt: Clicking a cluster label to filter the scatter plot and method table was a useful interaction.
What attributes matter most to you for deciding that two grasp planning methods are 'similar'? List the top 2-3.
response.placeholder: e.g., same gripper type, same sensor input, similar training paradigm, similar benchmark results...

response.prompt: What additional information or interaction would help you understand the scatter plot better?
response.placeholder: e.g., labels, legends, filters, different color schemes...

section2-explore.instruction:
**Section 2: Try the AI Copilot & Controls.**

Ask **2-3 questions** using the search bar.

After each query:
- Read the AI insight bullets
- Check if it cites specific papers
- Notice how the scatter plot and **weight sliders** update
- Try the **Color by** dropdown to see attributes like End-effector or Input Data
- **Scroll down** to see the analytics charts

Also try:
- Drag a **weight slider** to see how the scatter plot reorganizes
- Click a legend item to filter

**Think aloud**: tell us what's useful and what's confusing.

When done, click **Next**.

section2-feedback-with-tool.instruction:
**Rate the AI Copilot** while looking at the results.

Your last query results are still visible. Scroll through the insights, paper evidence, and charts as you answer these questions in the sidebar.

response.prompt: What queries did you ask?
response.placeholder: List the questions you tried...

response.prompt: The AI insights were relevant to my queries.
The insights contained accurate information.
The insights told me something new.
Paper citations helped me trust the claims.
I understood why the scatter plot changed.

section2-feedback-detail.instruction: A few more questions about the AI Copilot.

response.prompt: Did you notice any incorrect or fabricated information?
options: Possibly, but I am not sure

response.prompt: If you noticed inaccuracies, please describe them.
response.placeholder: Optional: what was incorrect?

response.prompt: What guardrails would you expect from an AI research tool? (Select all that apply)
options: Showing the exact passage each claim is based on
Ability to view the source PDF
Clear labeling when the AI is uncertain
Showing gaps in coverage (what the AI does not know)
Human expert review before displaying results
Ability to flag or correct wrong information

response.prompt: What are 2-3 questions you would genuinely want this tool to answer from these 34 papers — questions where you already know the correct answer and could judge whether the AI got it right?
response.placeholder: These become our evaluation test cases — we will run the AI on your questions and check its accuracy...

response.prompt: What would make the AI Copilot more useful?
response.placeholder: e.g., better answers, comparison mode, follow-up questions...

response.prompt: The weight sliders helped me explore the data in a way that was meaningful to my research.
The 'Color by' dropdown helped me see patterns across different attributes.
The analytics charts below the scatter plot (similarity scores, cited references, evidence breakdown) helped me understand the AI's answer.
Which analytics chart was most useful to you?
What additional visualizations, controls, or interactions would help you explore the grasp planning landscape?
response.placeholder: e.g., timeline, citation network, side-by-side comparison, different weight presets...

section3-explore.instruction:
**Section 3: Review the analytics dashboard.**

After asking a query, scroll down to see the analytics section. Look at:
- 'How This View Was Built' explanation
- Method relevance chart
- Cited references chart
- Topic cloud
- Evidence breakdown bars

**Think aloud**: tell us which charts are useful and which are confusing.

**Note:** You won't have access to the tool while answering questions next, so take a good look now.

When done, click **Next**.

response.prompt: 'How This View Was Built' helped me understand what the system did.
The 'Query-Method Similarity' chart was useful.
The 'Cited References' and 'Papers Referenced' charts were useful.
The 'Key Topics' cloud helped me understand the evidence.
The 'What Kind of Evidence?' breakdown (theory / implementation / evaluation) was useful.
Which analytics chart was most useful to you?
Which analytics chart was least useful or most confusing?
What additional visualizations or views would help you understand the grasp planning landscape?
response.placeholder: e.g., timeline, citation network, side-by-side comparison, method flowcharts...

section4-explore.instruction:
**Section 3: Explore the Knowledge Base.**

Click the **Knowledge Base** tab at the top of the tool.

- Browse the embedding scatter — each dot is a text chunk from a paper
- Click a paper chip on the right to highlight its chunks
- Try clicking a content type label (e.g., 'How it works') to filter
- Read a few chunk passages — are they readable and meaningful?

If you typed a query in Section 2, check whether the relevant papers are highlighted.

**Think aloud**: tell us whether the chunk view helps you understand the paper evidence.

**Note:** You won't have access to the tool while answering questions next, so take a good look now.

When done, click **Next**.

section4-feedback.instruction: Questions about the Knowledge Base page.

response.prompt: The scatter plot showing text chunks (each dot = one passage from a paper) was a useful way to explore the evidence.
The text passages shown were readable and captured meaningful content from the papers.
The content type labels (How it works / Implementation / Results) accurately described what was in the passages.
If you would categorize passages differently, how would you label them?
response.placeholder: e.g., different categories, finer distinctions, or 'the current ones are fine'...

response.prompt: When query results highlighted relevant papers in the Knowledge Base, it helped me trace where the AI's answer came from.
What information from a paper is most critical when someone is deciding whether to use a method — and is it captured in the chunks you saw?
response.placeholder: e.g., success rates, hardware requirements, failure modes, comparison results, code availability...

response.prompt: If you could search across all 34 papers for one thing — something you genuinely want to know — what would you ask?
response.placeholder: These become ground truth test queries for our RAG system...

response.prompt: If your own paper is in the knowledge base, did the chunks reflect its key contributions accurately?
options: Yes, the key points were captured
Partially — some important parts were missing
No, the chunks missed the main contributions
My paper is not in the knowledge base
I did not check my paper

response.prompt: If your paper's chunks were missing or incorrect, what was the most important thing they missed?
response.placeholder: Optional: describe what was missing or wrong...

comparison-spreadsheet.instruction:
**This is the current COMPARE spreadsheet** at robot-manipulation.org.

Spend 1-2 minutes browsing it. Try to:
- Find a method that uses reinforcement learning
- Compare two methods side by side
- Get an overview of the field

Think about how this compares to the Grasp Explorer you just used.

When done, click **Next**.

comparison-feedback.instruction: Compare the current spreadsheet with the Grasp Explorer tool you used earlier.

response.prompt: For FINDING a specific method, which is more effective?
For COMPARING methods against each other, which is more effective?
For DISCOVERING methods you did not know about, which is more effective?
For UNDERSTANDING the overall landscape of grasp planning, which is more effective?
For DIVING DEEP into a specific method's technical details, which is more effective?
If you could only use one tool for exploring grasp planning methods, which would you choose?
What does the spreadsheet do better than the Grasp Explorer?
response.placeholder: What would you miss if the spreadsheet were replaced?

response.prompt: What does the Grasp Explorer do better than the spreadsheet?
response.placeholder: What can you do with the Explorer that the spreadsheet cannot?

overall.instruction: Final questions about the overall tool.

response.prompt: Overall, how useful would this tool be for your research?
How likely are you to recommend this tool to a colleague?
How do you currently explore and compare grasp planning methods? (Select all that apply)
In your opinion, what is the most effective way to explore a collection of 56+ research papers?
response.placeholder: Describe your ideal workflow or tool...

response.prompt: How would you ideally want to interact with and visualize this kind of research data?
response.placeholder: Describe interactions, views, or features you would want...

response.prompt: What single improvement would make this tool most valuable to you?
response.placeholder: The one change that would have the biggest impact...

response.prompt: What concerns, if any, do you have about using AI-generated insights for research decisions?
response.placeholder: e.g., accuracy, bias, over-reliance, missing context...

response.prompt: What data about grasp planning methods is missing from the current tool that you wish were tracked or searchable?
response.placeholder: e.g., real-world success rates, hardware cost, inference speed, open-source availability, specific failure modes...

response.prompt: Imagine a new student joins your lab and needs to choose a grasp planning method. What 3 questions should this tool be able to answer for them?
response.placeholder: These define the core use cases the tool should handle...

response.prompt: The COMPARE ecosystem covers 6 categories: Grasp Planning, Motion Planning, Perception, Learning, Simulation, and Pipelines. Which would you most want this tool for next?
For the landscape you selected above, would the same layout (scatter + table + AI query + knowledge base) work, or would it need a different view?
response.placeholder: e.g., same layout works / would need different columns / different chunk types / different clustering attributes...

response.prompt: Any other feedback, suggestions, or comments?
response.placeholder: Optional
